title: Electronic structure computations of multi-million atom. Non-periodic samples of metal oxides in less than 10 minutes? Yes.
author: Michalewicz Marek
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Equation of motion method has been used previously for computations of the electronic conductivity via Kubo formula, localization, spectral functions, interband linear and non-linear optical properties, the Hall coefficient, the electronic structure of hydrogenated a-Si and the effective mass of electron and hole in amorphous silicon. Host of other properties in complex sytems could be explored computationally with programs based on equation-of-motion method and the tight-binding Hamiltonian: total energy computations10, electronic structure of elemental materials, transition and noble metals. It is likely that modelling of the entire monolithic electronic device will be possible with the use of extremelly fast computer codes. In materials research we may envisage very fast computational materials “discovery” process (a “virtual” counterpart to continous composition-spread method type of a process). In solid state physics and materials sciences a method described here and its extensions can be invaluable for studying the systems for which very large supercells make ab-initio methods (still) impractical, systems such as (modulated) superlatices, surfaces, atomic steps, islands, interfaces, extended surface defects, random alloys, sol-gel systems, sintered nanocrystalline materials or carbon nanostructures. Some of the immediate extensions and enhancements worth looking at are: positional relaxation, tight-binding Molecular Dynamics (TBMD), nonorthogonal tight-binding schemes and combination of methods described here with ab-initio schemes.
